references.title: Transformer Encoder Model for Sequential Prediction of Student Performance Based on Their Log Activities

extracted.Data sources: VLE logs, Learner Characteristics, Course Information
extracted.Features: [id_student, code_module, code_presentation, activity_type, score, is_banked, weight, sum_click, date]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The original table was arranged in a way that the original table was converted into a 4-dimensional tensor: 

(N, D, T, F)

D accounts Days and is of length 270 (full duration). 

Authors also attempt a weekdly aggregation instead of a daily one. 

Authors use 2 layers of Transformational encoders with 100 units each. 

Another notable distinction is the use of a Weighted Loss Function instead of the Standard Binary Cross-Entropy
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose a new architecture to do prediction of End of Course Performance. The proposed architecture uses a Transformer model.

Results on different binary tasks show good results, with daily representations winning. However… these results seem low compared to other methods I’ve seen using OULAD.
extracted.Limitations:
• No other architectures are tested to benchmark against the one used 

  • One Dataset

  • No multiclass setting

  • Only considers Activity Features

annotations.text: The authors propose a new deep learning model to predict student performance using the sequential aspect of interaction logs between a student and an LMS in the hopes of providing support as early as possible.

The authors use OULAD to perform multiple early classification tasks and their proposed model achieves, at the very least, 76% when performing early prediction. When comparing the performance of the Transformer-Encoder model, the authors found that their proposed method outperformed the baselkine LSTM models by 3% to 7% on F1-Score.

Authors also test the effect of different pre-processing options such as positional encoding. In OULAD, the best options had no positional encoding and, in some cases, worked better with weeky feature aggragation and weighted loss functions.
